skill.name: nano-banana-edit
skill.description: Edit existing images with Google Nano Banana 2 through the RunComfy CLI. The skill documents input schema, prompting patterns, batch editing, output options, and model-routing guidance.
skill.summary: Run Nano Banana 2 image edits through RunComfy with schema-aware prompts and batch workflows.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The high static score is mostly caused by Markdown backticks, placeholder URLs, and documentation examples rather than hidden executable code. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill intentionally directs agents to run the RunComfy CLI, send prompts and image URLs to a third-party model API, and use local token and output files.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to invoke the RunComfy CLI and provides install, login, and run commands. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it requires the user to trust the local CLI and command arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command behavior is explicitly documented and repeated in invocation examples. No hidden script is present, but external command execution is intentional.

medium_findings.title: Prompts and Image URLs Are Sent to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The documented workflow submits the JSON body to the RunComfy model API and uses publicly fetchable image URLs. Users should avoid sensitive images, private URLs, or confidential prompt text unless their RunComfy policy allows it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file states that the CLI POSTs to a specific external model API and that input image URLs are fetched. This confirms third-party data handling.

medium_findings.title: Local Token and Output File Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents a token file under the user configuration directory and downloads generated outputs into the selected output directory. This is expected behavior, but users should protect tokens and choose output locations carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and output directory behavior are directly documented. The token file mode is described as owner-only, reducing but not eliminating local secret-handling risk.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Alerts Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but the referenced lines are Markdown inline code, fenced command examples, schema tables, and sample prompts. No standalone executable script or dynamic shell expansion was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation, not Ruby code. The actual command examples are already captured as intentional CLI behavior in medium findings.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URL Path Traversal Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections point to placeholder image URLs that contain ellipses, such as sample HTTPS image locations. They are not filesystem paths and do not instruct traversal of local directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences appear inside example HTTPS URLs in JSON snippets. There is no local file read or relative path traversal operation.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance flags correspond to prose or headings, not cryptographic code, host inspection, or environment enumeration. No evidence found for malicious cryptography or system reconnaissance in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are front matter, routing prose, and an exit-code heading. They do not contain cryptographic APIs or reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: RunComfy CLI Invocation With User-Supplied JSON
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill centers on `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit` with a JSON input body. The documentation says prompt content is transmitted directly to the Model API and is not shell-expanded, which lowers injection risk but still requires trusted CLI execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The invocation and stated input boundary are explicit. Risk depends on the installed CLI and the user-provided URLs and prompt content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Model API Submission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents outbound submission to the RunComfy model API and generated output downloads from RunComfy domains. This is not hidden exfiltration, but it is a clear third-party data transfer pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound domains and behavior are documented in the security section. This confirms network exposure but not malicious intent.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Nano Banana on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Image teams need fast edits that preserve identity, framing, and brand details. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through RunComfy Nano Banana 2 image edits.
actual_capabilities: Runs the RunComfy Nano Banana 2 edit endpoint through the local CLI.
Documents the required prompt and image_urls schema for image edits.
Supports one to twenty input images for batch or reference-based edits.
Explains output count, resolution, aspect ratio, format, seed, and safety options.
Provides prompting guidance for identity preservation, background swaps, and spatial edits.
Compares when to use Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 edit, Flux Kontext, or text-to-image.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid authentication.
Sends prompts and image URLs to the RunComfy model API over the network.
Needs publicly fetchable HTTPS image URLs for input images.
Long compound edit requests may drift and should be split into smaller passes.

use_cases.title: Create Product Gallery Variants
use_cases.description: Batch edit product images with consistent framing, resolution, and background style.

use_cases.title: Refresh Campaign Visuals
use_cases.description: Preserve a spokesperson or product while changing backgrounds, palettes, or localized details.

use_cases.title: Prototype Visual Edits From Chat
use_cases.description: Turn a natural language edit request into a RunComfy CLI call with the correct schema.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana Edit to keep the subject identity and pose unchanged. Change only the background to a clean studio setting.

prompt_templates.title: Product Batch Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Consistent edits for several product images
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana Edit for these product image URLs. Keep each product unchanged, lock a 1:1 aspect ratio, and create a soft grey studio background.

prompt_templates.title: Targeted Local Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana Edit to remove only the object in the upper-right corner. Keep all other objects, lighting, shadows, and framing unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Multi-Pass Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a two-pass Nano Banana Edit workflow. First change only the background, then adjust only the clothing color while preserving identity and composition.

output_examples.input: A user asks to place a portrait subject on a rainy neon street while keeping the face and clothing unchanged.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Nano Banana Edit run with a preservation-first prompt, one image URL, and an output directory for the generated image.

output_examples.input: A product manager provides three SKU image URLs and asks for matching square studio images.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses one batch edit request, locks the aspect ratio and resolution, and applies the same background instruction to every input.

output_examples.input: A designer asks whether to use Nano Banana Edit for multilingual label replacement.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a better text-editing model when precise multilingual in-image text is the main requirement.

best_practices: Start prompts with what must stay unchanged, then state the requested edit clearly.
Lock aspect ratio and resolution when editing a batch of related images.
Use small multi-pass edits when one prompt tries to change several unrelated elements.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential images or private URLs unless the third-party processing policy allows it.
Do not combine many unrelated edit goals in one long prompt.
Do not rely on vague instructions such as make it better or change the style.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an assistant to run Nano Banana 2 image edits through the RunComfy CLI.

faq.question: Does it generate images from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It is for image-to-image edits using one or more existing image URLs.

faq.question: How many input images can it use?
faq.answer: The documented endpoint accepts one to twenty input images per call.

faq.question: What data leaves my machine?
faq.answer: The prompt and image URLs are submitted to the RunComfy model API for processing.

faq.question: Does it store an API token locally?
faq.answer: The skill documents RunComfy CLI token storage under the user configuration directory after login.

faq.question: When should I choose another model?
faq.answer: Use another model when precise multilingual text editing or text-to-image generation is the main task.
